coredata.dc:title: Mechanistic studies on the reversible photophysical properties of carbon nanodots at different pH
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Zi-Qiang
Lan, Jia-Yi
Jin, Jian-Cheng
Gao, Tian
Pan, Ling-Li
Jiang, Feng-Lei
Liu, Yi
coredata.dc:description: The pH-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior of carbon nanodots (C-dots) and its mechanism has been exhaustively studied in this work. The PL and UV–vis absorption spectra are reversible in the pH between 3 and 13. We speculate that two kinds of reactions (fast and slow) occurring at the surface of C-dots may contribute to this pH-dependent PL behavior. When C-dots solutions are switched to acidic conditions, they will quickly self-assembled aggregate into larger particles and surface oxygen-related groups of C-dots would be slowly oxidized at room temperature. Moreover, it should be noted that this is the first direct observation of self-assembled aggregation of C-dots under acidic conditions. In addition, the optimal PL spectra of C-dots blue-shift while their sizes increase, so-called ‘inverse PL shift’ phenomenon is also observed. Meanwhile, as the solution is adjusted to alkaline conditions, a structural tautomerization of C-dots rapidly takes place and hydrogenation/deoxygenation reaction proceeds in a much slower rate. Furthermore, through distinct decay dynamics as well as the characterizations of C-dots at different pHs, the PL properties are proposed to be mainly related to the surface states of C-dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
pH dependent PL
Mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of C-dots at different pH values

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–c) PL spectra of A C-dots, N C-dots and B C-dots. The excitation wavelengths varied from 320 to 500nm with 20nm increments as indicated. (d) The optimal emission and corresponding excitation wavelengths of C-dots obtained at different pH from 2 to 13. Error bars represent standard error of the mean (n =3).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of A C-dots, N C-dots and B C-dots. Inset: photograph of three solutions containing the C-dots (from left to right, A C-dots, N C-dots and B C-dots). Some off-sets have been given for a clear presentation of figures. (b) The optimal PL spectra of A C-dots (excited by 390nm), N C-dots (excited by 420nm) and B C-dots (excited by 420nm) in water.

(a, b) UV–vis absorption spectra of the C-dots as a function of the pH between 3 and 13 at an interval of 2. The process is reversible. Some offsets have been given for a clear presentation of figures. (c) pH dependent PL spectra of the C-dots when pH is switched between 3 and 13 through 7. Inset: dependence of PL intensity on pH (changed from 2 to 13 and then from 13 to 2). (d) Dependence of PL intensity on different storage time (from 1h to 16 days). Error bars represent standard error of the mean (n =3).

Morphology images of the three types of C-dots. (a–c) TEM images of A C-dots, N C-dots and B C-dots, respectively. (d–f) HR-TEM images A C-dots, N C-dots and B C-dots, respectively. (g–i) Size distributions of A C-dots, N C-dots and B C-dots, respectively. 100–200 particles were counted for statistic analysis.
(a–c) Fitted C1s high resolution XPS spectra of A C-dots (a), N C-dots (b) and B C-dots(c). (d) FTIR spectra of A C-dots, N C-dots and B C-dots.
Schemes for the mechanism of pH dependent optical behavior for C-dots.
Area percentages of deconvoluted peaks in the total C1s XPS spectra of A, N and B-dots samples.

The average lifetimes of C-dots detected at different emission wavelengths when excited at 337nm. I(t)= α 1exp(−t/τ 1)+ α 2exp(−t/τ 2), τ average =(α 1 τ 1 2 + α 2 τ 2 2)/(α 1 τ 1+ α 2 τ 2). The standard error is the result of three reproducibility measurements (n =3).

head.ce:title: Mechanistic studies on the reversible photophysical properties of carbon nanodots at different pH

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: State Key Laboratory of Virology & Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (MOE), College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, PR China. Tel.: +86 27 68756667; fax: +86 27 68754067.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The pH-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior of carbon nanodots (C-dots) and its mechanism has been exhaustively studied in this work. The PL and UV–vis absorption spectra are reversible in the pH between 3 and 13. We speculate that two kinds of reactions (fast and slow) occurring at the surface of C-dots may contribute to this pH-dependent PL behavior. When C-dots solutions are switched to acidic conditions, they will quickly self-assembled aggregate into larger particles and surface oxygen-related groups of C-dots would be slowly oxidized at room temperature. Moreover, it should be noted that this is the first direct observation of self-assembled aggregation of C-dots under acidic conditions. In addition, the optimal PL spectra of C-dots blue-shift while their sizes increase, so-called ‘inverse PL shift’ phenomenon is also observed. Meanwhile, as the solution is adjusted to alkaline conditions, a structural tautomerization of C-dots rapidly takes place and hydrogenation/deoxygenation reaction proceeds in a much slower rate. Furthermore, through distinct decay dynamics as well as the characterizations of C-dots at different pHs, the PL properties are proposed to be mainly related to the surface states of C-dots.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanomaterials have become the focus of advanced scientific researches as they possess a variety of fascinating properties. Carbon nanodots (C-dots) have attracted great attention in carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials including nanodiamonds [1], fluorescent carbon nanotubes [2] and fullerene [3], due to their superior physical and chemical properties [4]. Compared with conventional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) and organic dyes, C-dots possess more outstanding properties, such as superior photoluminescence (PL) properties, favorable biocompatibility, chemical inertness and easy functionalization [5]. Thus, their performance is competitive to the commercially available Cd, Hg and Pb containing QDs [6,7]. Therefore, these interesting properties make them have potential applications in bioimaging [8–10], light-emitting diodes [11], sensors [12–14], drug delivery [15], gene delivery [16], solar cells [17], photocatalysis [18,19] and mimetics peroxidase [20]. C-dots exhibit several other interesting PL properties, such as excitation wavelength (λ ex) dependent emission and up-conversion property, which make them quite different from the conventional QDs [5,6]. In general, highly luminescent C-dots can be achieved through various strategies [21–23], surface passivation [24], doping [7], coupling to metal-based nanostructures [25] and reducing [26]. Previous studies indicate that the origin of PL in C-dots and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) may arise from quantum size effect, zig–zag sites and defect states (energy traps) [4,27]. Generally, quantum size effect and zig–zag sites can be considered as intrinsic state emission while the defect effect is classified as emissive traps. In addition, the intrinsic state emission stems from recombination of localized electron–hole pairs [28].

The PL behavior of C-dots has been extensively studied through steady-state and time-resolved PL methods. So far, though much progress has been achieved in the synthesis of highly luminescent C-dots, many issues need to be addressed before we can apply them in practice. The mechanism of PL is still under debate, varying from case to case. However, it is very important for achieving highly luminescent C-dots.

The pH-dependent PL behavior is one of the most important properties existed in C-dots [23,29]. Pan et al. [30] reported that C-dots emitted strong PL under alkaline conditions, while the PL was nearly completely quenched in acidic conditions. Interestingly, the PL intensity could be restored or quenched by switching pH between 13 and 1, while emission wavelength (λ em) was not affected by pH. In parallel, Wang and co-workers [31] reported that when 1.28molL−1 HNO3 was added, a new PL sites emerges with an optimal λ ex at 350nm and an optimal λ em 500nm, respectively. They attributed this unusual emission transformation to self-assembled aggregation of GQDs. Unfortunately, they did not directly observe the self-assembled aggregation of GQDs by HRTEM. In this work, a similar unusual PL transformation under a milder acidic condition had also been observed, and self-assembled aggregation of C-dots was also directly observed by HRTEM.

To date, the mechanism of reversible pH-dependent PL behavior for C-dots has not been exhaustively studied. Herein, we report the pH-dependent PL behavior of C-dots in detail and explore the possible reason for this interesting phenomenon. The proposed pH-dependent mechanism can provide unique insights into the origin of PL. The findings indicate that pH-dependent PL behavior is associated with two kinds of reactions (fast and slow) occurring at the surface of C-dots. This result further proves that the PL of C-dots is derived from the surface defects states.

Sucrose, phosphoric acid (H3PO4), hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (China). All reagents were used as received. The aqueous solutions were prepared with ultra-pure water (18.2MΩcm−1, Millipore).
The C-dots were prepared by acidic oxidation methods based on our previous report with some modifications [32]. The modifications were given in supporting information.

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were recorded on a JEOL JEM-2100 (HR) electron microscope operating at 200kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out on ESCLAB 250Xi using Al as the exciting source under CAE mode. FTIR spectra of C-dots were recorded as KBr pellets with a Nicolet 360 FTIR spectrophotometer operating from 4000 to 500cm−1. Fluorescence analysis was performed on LS-55 fluorophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA) in the ratio mode. UNICO 4802 UV-vis double-beam spectrophotometer was used to measure absorption spectra of C-dots. Fluorescence intensity decay curves were measured on a FELIX32 system (Photon Technology International).

The C-dots were prepared by one-pot acidic oxidation methods with some modifications based on our previous report [32]. The as-prepared C-dots were green-emitting with relatively high oxygen content and the PL behavior were pH dependent (Fig. S1), just like the C-dots prepared by other methods [29,30]. Although the PL intensity of C-dots kept constant in the pH range between 7 and 10, PL intensity notably decreased out of this range.

Excitation dependent PL behavior is very common in C-dots [16,23]. In this work, the excitation dependent PL behavior at different pHs was shown in Fig. 1. The solution pH is adjusted to 3, 7, and 13 using either HCl or NaOH, these three kinds of C-dots containing solutions are denoted as acidic C-dots (A C-dots), neutral C-dots (N C-dots) and basic C-dots (B C-dots), respectively. Obviously, the three kinds of C-dots showed a gradual evolution of PL spectra, where a shoulder peak and a peak are observed excited under shorter excitation wavelengths but new features appeared at longer excitation wavelengths. With increasing of excitation wavelength (320–500nm at an interval of 20nm), the shoulder peak at ∼448nm (peak centered at ∼440nm for B C-dots) gradually disappeared while the peak at ∼490nm increased remarkably and λ em was red-shifted (Fig. 1 a–c). Noteworthy, the shape and intensity ratio of peak as well as shoulder peak were different between each other when excited at 320nm. The movement of the λ em relative to the λ ex for different C-dots has been shown in Fig. S2. Moreover, as the pH increased from 2 to 13, the optimal λ em and λ ex of C-dots were both red-shifted (Fig. 1d), indicating that the C-dots would have different energy levels at different pHs, corresponding to the different maximum transition probability [33].

All the three kinds of C-dots displayed typical UV absorption bands centered at ∼280nm which was likely originated from the n–π* electronic transition of the CO bond [34]. Interestingly, an additional absorption band at ∼220nm was observed for A C-dots and N C-dots, while it was disappeared for B C-dots (Fig. 2a). The peak at 220nm could derive from π–π* transition of conjugated CC band and changes of C-dots’ surface states might lead to the alterations of the absorption bands [34]. Moreover, the color of solution was darkened from light yellow to yellow-brown with the increasing of pH (insets of Fig. 2 a). The optimal PL spectra of three C-dots were also presented (Fig. 2b). The N C-dots exhibited optimal λ ex and λ em at 420 and 525nm, respectively. The λ ex and λ em of B C-dots were similar to those of N C-dots while they were blue-shifted to 390 and 502nm for A C-dots. Noteworthy, previous studies reported that the pH value usually had nearly no effect on λ em of C-dots, but it only affected the PL intensities [23]. In addition, the maximum PL intensity of the N C-dots was larger than the rest two types of C-dots. The PL intensity of B C-dots was weaker than that of A C-dots.

The optical properties including UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of the C-dots change reversibly with the variation of the pH in the range of 3–13 (Fig. 3). The absorption band at 280nm is red-shifted ∼5nm with the increase of pH. The peak centered at 220nm gradually becomes ill-defined as the pH increases from 3 to 11 and finally disappears at higher pH (Fig. 3 a). Upon decreasing the pH to 3, the absorption spectra recover its original shape, including both the 220 and 280nm absorption bands. Indeed, upon decreasing the pH, the peak at 220nm becomes perceptible again at pH 11, then better defined as the pH continues to decline to 3 (Fig. 3b). In addition, the λ em is red-shifted ∼20nm and the PL intensity increases obviously when the pH is switched from 3 to 7. As the pH is adjusted from 7 to 13, the PL intensity decays while the λ em is not shifted. Moreover, when the pH is restored to 3, both λ em and PL intensity of C-dots are restored (Fig. 3c). These results demonstrate that the PL behavior of C-dots is unambiguously reversible in the pH range of 3–13.

The sustainability of this reversibility is also investigated (Fig. 3d). Correspondingly, the PL spectra of C-dots storing in acidic/alkaline conditions for 16 days or when they are switched back to neutral conditions after storing in acidic/alkaline conditions for 16 days are also provided (Fig. S3). Indeed, both the PL intensity and λ em can be reversibly restored when the C-dots are stored in acidic/alkaline conditions for 16 days. Interestingly, when C-dots are kept in acidic/alkaline conditions for a long period, the PL intensity increases with the storage time prolonged. This behavior indicates the presence of a slow structural transformation occurring at the surface of C-dots during the storage period that affects the PL properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of C-dots at different pH values
ce:section.ce:para: Several characterization techniques are employed to characterize the sizes and structure of the C-dots at different pHs. The morphology and size distribution of three C-dots are characterized by TEM (Fig. 4). All the C-dots are quasispherical and monodisperse (Fig. 4 a–c). The histograms show that the size distribution of the N C-dots, B C-dots and A C-dots is about 2.7±1.2, 3.1±1.5 and 4.7±1.5nm, respectively (Fig. 4g–i). For the A C-dots, it is suspected that the C-dots self-assemble into larger particles upon switching pH of solution from neutral to acidic conditions. Intuitively, the oxygen-related groups functionalized on graphite core facilitate intermolecular hydrogen-bond interaction, which entails molecular stacking and consequently favors charge transport [35]. This is the first direct observation of self-assembled aggregation of C-dots under acidic conditions. Concurrently, the optimal PL spectra are blue-shifted while size increases, which is described by a so-called ‘inverse PL shift’ phenomenon. Furthermore, HRTEM images show that all the C-dots have crystallinity with lattice spacings of 0.21–0.34nm (Fig. 4d–f). These lattices distances may be sp2 clusters in C-dots obtained during oxidation [4].

The XPS spectra showed that the degrees of oxidation increased in a sequence of B C-dots<N C-dots<A C-dots (Figs. S4 and S5). All C1s spectra can be deconvoluted into four surface components (Fig. 5a–c) and the relative content of four surface components is listed in Table 1 . The content of oxygen-related groups at the surface of A C-dots were larger than the rest two types of C-dots and the relative content of carbonyl groups in B C-dots is smaller than that of N C-dots while the content of hydroxyl groups is larger than that of N C-dots. This result can also be confirmed by FTIR characterization. In the FTIR spectra, the absorption bands at 3423, 1635, 1386 and 1055cm−1 which can be assigned to ν OH, ν CO, δ CH and ν CO, respectively (Fig. 5 d). The intensity of ν OH, ν CO and ν CO bands relative to that of δCH descended as A C-dots>N C-dots>B C-dots, indicating that some kinds of reactions occur at the surface of C-dots. These findings corroborate the results of the XPS characterization.

There are several novel and interesting phenomena needed to be addressed, including ‘inverse PL shift’ phenomenon, the short-period reversible optical properties and long-period PL enhancement behavior (Fig. 6 ).

Indeed, when the solutions is adjusted to acidic conditions, the size of C-dots increased about 1.74 times (from 2.7 to 4.7nm) while the optimal λ em are blue-shifted ∼20nm, so-called ‘inverse PL shift’ phenomenon is also observed. The mechanism of the ‘inverse PL shift’ is still debatable [36]. Rhee et al. speculated that the surface oleyamine ligands of C-dots as aucochromes might donate their lone pairs of electrons to the LUMOs of the sp2 clusters, and then energy gaps between π and π* states of sp2 clusters would be reduced. As the larger C-dots bare more sp2 clusters free from surface ligands (less or no electron donation effect), such energy gap reduction effect is less pronounced which may ultimately attribute to so-called ‘inverse PL shift’ phenomenon [37]. As for our work, when the pH is switched to 3, the C-dots self-assemble into larger particles by intermolecular hydrogen-bond interaction. Compared with N C-dots, the surface oxygen-related groups of larger particles (A C-dots) donate their electrons to the LUMOs of the sp2 clusters less effectively. Then the energy gap reduction effect decreases, which lead to the blue-shift of optimal λ em. This ‘ligand-less’ effect can also influence the absorption properties, the absorption peak at 280nm is blue-shifted ∼5nm when the pH is adjusted from 7 to 3 (Fig. 2a).

Several groups reported that when the pH of C-dots is switched repeatedly between 13 and 1, the PL intensity varied reversibly while the λ em is not affected by pHs. Pan et al. [30] attributed this reversible phenomenon to the formation of a reversible complex between zig–zag sites and H+. In our work, both PL intensity and λ em varied reversibly with the alteration of pH. We speculated that the reversible PL behavior between pH of 7 and 13 may be ascribed to the formation of structural tautomer, similar to the isomerization between phenolic hydroxyl and quinone residues as the core of C-dots can be viewed as a large conjugated system. This is similar to reversible complex between zig–zag sites and H+ mentioned above. The appearance of the darkening of the solution can be understood by the formation of reversible isomerization. Accordingly, the self-assembled aggregation of C-dots in acidic conditions as well as subsequent isomerization behavior in alkaline conditions may contribute to the reversible properties of C-dots between pH 3 and 13.

Another interesting behavior need to be addressed is PL enhancement phenomenon under long period. We speculate that the enhancement of PL for C-dots when they are stored in acidic/alkaline conditions may be ascribed to slow reactions occurring under acidic/alkaline conditions. Guo and co-workers [34] reported that the carbonyl groups of C-dots can be partly reduced to hydroxyl when they were stored in very strong alkaline conditions (2molL−1 NaOH). These reactions are quite similar to the deoxygenation of exfoliated graphite oxide (GO) [38]. Concurrently, several other groups reported that the surface oxygen-related groups of C-dots can be oxidized under strong acidic conditions at room temperature [30,31,39]. So we think that the hydrogenation/deoxygenation and oxidation of C-dots can also proceed under relatively milder alkaline and acidic conditions with a much slower rate. The structural changes identified by FTIR and XPS provide evidence for the presence of hydrogenation/deoxygenation and oxidation reactions for the C-dots.

So far, most studies attributed the PL of C-dots to differently sized nanoparticles (quantum effect) and/or surface-defects existed in the C-dots or a mechanism currently unresolved. For instance, various oxygen-containing groups introduced onto the surface of C-dots can act as surface states with the energy levels between π and π* states of CC and induce absorption bands due to the electron transition within one or several of these groups [40]. Both two contrary processes (oxidation in acidic condition and deoxygenation in alkaline conditions) would increase the content of different oxygen-related groups, then it would increase the efficiency of electron transition within these groups. So the two contrary processes (oxidation in acidic condition and deoxygenation in alkaline conditions) that show the same effect on the PL of C-dots can be understand in this way. Moreover, Zheng et al. [26] reported the synthesis of reduced state carbon dots by reducing carbon dot with NaBH4. The QY of the reduced state carbon dots increased from 2% to 24%. Meanwhile, Tan et al. [41] studied the effect of UV light irradiation on the PL of C-dots, the content of carbonyl groups increases (oxidation) significantly with the increase of the irradiation time, which result in the enhancement of green emission. So the enhancement of PL for C-dots may stem from these reactions.

Moreover, understanding the mechanism of pH-dependent PL behavior can offer valuable insights into the origin of PL for C-dots. In order to reveal the intrinsic PL mechanism, the relationship between the changes of chemical structure and corresponding PL properties is investigated. So far, most studies attributed the PL of the C-dots to intrinsic state (quantum size effect and zig–zag sites) and defect states.

It is obvious that the changes of surface functional groups may better account for the reversibility and long-period PL enhancement behavior rather than the size. In other words, various surface functional groups could act as surface states to traps the excitons under excitations, and radiative recombination of these surface-trapped excitons would give the emission with corresponding energy. The intrinsic PL mechanism could be further demonstrated by the time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy. A typical PL decay curve of the C-dots excited at 337nm is displayed in Fig. S6. The PL decay curve of the C-dots is biphasic with short-lived component τ 1 and long-lived component τ 2. It is similar to that of luminescent silicon nanoparticles, including fast bandgap transition and long decay of surface state related emission [42]. The PL dynamics could be investigated through the average lifetime (τ average) of the C-dots.

As shown in Table 2 , the τ average of A C-dots at all the emission wavelength are shorter than that of N C-dots while the sizes are much larger. A C-dots with larger sizes and ‘less ligand’ would relax more via ‘internal’ energy transfer, corresponding to less complicated surface states (such as oxygen-related groups) to afford shorter lifetimes [37]. Meanwhile, as for the B C-dots, the τ average became shorter as the monitoring wavelength increases. This behavior is similar to the trend of N C-dots while surface oxidation degrees of B C-dots are lower. A similar dynamic trend has also been observed in previous reports [33]. They attributed the intrinsic PL mechanism of C-dots to different oxidation degrees. Moreover, the variation of τ average for B C-dots at different emission wavelength is significantly smaller than those for N C-dots (relative standard deviation of average lifetimes, RSDN C-dots =59.4% and RSDB C-dots =48.1%). In other words, the surface-trapped excitons of the B C-dots would experience less complex de-excitation pathways, corresponding to less complicated surface states to generate various fluorescence lifetimes [43]. All these distinct decay dynamics besides the characterizations pronounced above suggesting that the surface states of C-dots were mainly responsible for the PL.

The pH-dependent PL properties of the C-dots and the possible mechanism for this interesting phenomenon have been exhaustively studied in this work. The PL as well as UV–vis absorption spectra can be reversibly switched between pH range of 3 and 13. Speculatively, the pH-dependent reversibility of the PL behavior is associated with two kinds of reactions (fast and slow) occurring at the surface of C-dots. When the pH is switched from 7 to 3, the C-dots will quickly self-assemble into larger particles and surface oxygen-related groups of C-dots can be slowly oxidized at room temperature. Moreover, an inverse PL shift is also observed in acidic conditions. Concurrently, as the pH is switched from 7 to 13, a structural tautomer seems to be formed very fast and hydrogenation/deoxygenation reaction proceed in a much slower rate. In addition, the surface states of C-dots are possibly mainly responsible for the PL through fluorescent decay dynamics as well as characterization of the C-dots at different pHs. This work exhibits a unique opportunity to explore the intrinsic PL mechanism of C-dots and provides guidance to obtain C-dots with more specific pH-dependent properties toward potential practical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from Chinese 973 Program (Grant No. 2011CB933600), National Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars of China (Grant No. 21225313), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21303126, 21473125), Hubei Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 3014CFA003), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2042014kf0287). The authors thank Prof. Z.K. He and Dr. C.L. Zhang of Wuhan University for helping with the characterization of fluorescence decay curves and Prof. Pierre D. Harvey of Université de Sherbrooke for giving valuable suggestions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2015.04.012.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The properties and applications of nanodiamonds
A route to brightly fluorescent carbon nanotubes for near-infrared imaging in mice
Luminescent hollow carbon shells and fullerene-like carbon spheres produced by laser ablation with toluene
Surface chemistry routes to modulate the photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots: from fluorescence mechanism to up-conversion bioimaging applications
Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
Doped carbon nanoparticles as a new platform for highly photoluminescent dots
Carbon quantum dots for optical bioimaging
One-pot hydrothermal synthesis of highly luminescent nitrogen-doped amphoteric carbon dots for bioimaging from Bombyx mori silk – natural proteins
Large scale synthesis of photoluminescent carbon nanodots and their application for bioimaging
Color-switchable electroluminescence of carbon dot light-emitting diodes
A sensitive and reliable dopamine biosensor was developed based on the Au@carbon dots–chitosan composite film
Carbon dots and chitosan composite film based biosensor for the sensitive and selective determination of dopamine
Ag Nanoparticle-decorated graphene quantum dots for label-free, rapid and sensitive detection of Ag+ and biothiols
Hollow luminescent carbon dots for drug delivery
Nano-carrier for gene delivery and bioimaging based on carbon dots with PEI-passivation enhanced fluorescence
Solution phase synthesis of carbon quantum dots as sensitizers for nanocrystalline TiO2 solar cells
Near-infrared light controlled photocatalytic activity of carbon quantum dots for highly selective oxidation reaction
Carbon quantum dots/Cu2O composites with protruding nanostructures and their highly efficient (near) infrared photocatalytic behavior
Carbon nanodots as peroxidase mimetics and their applications to glucose detection
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging
One-step ultrasonic synthesis of water-soluble carbon nanoparticles with excellent photoluminescent properties
Focusing on luminescent graphene quantum dots: current status and future perspectives
Photoluminescent carbon dots as biocompatible nanoprobes for targeting cancer cells in vitro
Enhancing the luminescence of carbon dots with a reduction pathway
Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
Fabrication and characterization of beaded SiC quantum rings with anomalous red spectral shift
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles: electrochemical synthesis and their pH sensitive photoluminescence properties
Hydrothermal route for cutting graphene sheets into blue-luminescent graphene quantum dots
Unusual emission transformation of graphene quantum dots induced by self-assembled aggregation
Low temperature synthesis of highly stable phosphate functionalized two color carbon nanodots and their application in cell imaging
Electrochemical tuning of luminescent carbon nanodots: from preparation to luminescence mechanism
Chemiluminescence of carbon dots under strong alkaline solutions: a novel insight into carbon dot optical properties
π-Conjugated cyanostilbene derivatives: a unique self-assembly motif for molecular nanostructures with enhanced emission and transport
Preparation of carbon nanodots from single chain polymeric nanoparticles and theoretical investigation of the photoluminescence mechanism
Size-controlled soft-template synthesis of carbon nanodots toward versatile photoactive materials
Deoxygenation of exfoliated graphite oxide under alkaline conditions: a green route to graphene preparation
How graphene is cut upon oxidation?
Graphene quantum dots: an emerging material for energy-related applications and beyond
Effect of UV irradiation on photoluminescence of carbon dots
Size-dependent intrinsic radiative decay rates of silicon nanocrystals at large confinement energies
Shifting and non-shifting fluorescence emitted by carbon nanodots
